title: Building End-to-End

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's enterprise platform where full stack engineers build end-to-end product experiences working directly with users to solve complex data problems

extractedEntities.name: Full Stack Engineering
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's engineering model where engineers own entire product experiences end-to-end, from database to UI, rather than splitting between frontend and backend

extractedEntities.name: End-to-End Ownership
extractedEntities.description: Organizational pattern where engineers take complete ownership of complex problems including product thinking, design, architecture, and direct user engagement

extractedEntities.name: Forward Deployment
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's model of deploying engineers directly to work alongside customers to solve real-world problems and iterate on product experiences

extractedEntities.name: Full Stack Product Development
extractedEntities.description: Engineers working across the entire application stack — from infrastructure to user interface — to deliver complete product experiences driven by real user needs

keyInsights: Palantir's full stack engineering extends beyond frontend/backend split to encompass complete product ownership including design, architecture, and user engagement
Engineers have significant autonomy to choose their work and make architectural decisions, with trust extended even to new hires
The engineering culture emphasizes flexibility — engineers may specialize in frontend on one project and backend on the next, adapting to where they are needed most

title: Data Connection: The first step in data integration (Palantir RFx Blog Series, #2)

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: A utility that copies, moves, or virtualizes data from a source system to the pipeline environment of a data ecosystem on a persistent basis, handling various source types, frequencies, volumes, and governance policies

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data platform that provides comprehensive data integration capabilities including secure data connections to diverse source systems

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection Agent
extractedEntities.description: A deployable agent or service that can be placed into source systems to establish secure data connections where direct network egress is not possible or represents a security concern

extractedEntities.name: Data Integration Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: A transformation pipeline where incremental changes are applied to raw data — connecting, harmonizing, securing, and transporting it into a new integrated system

extractedEntities.name: Source System
extractedEntities.description: An origin data system from which data connections pull data, which can be structured, unstructured, semi-structured, batch, or streaming

extractedEntities.name: Data Governance Policy
extractedEntities.description: Security, retention, and purpose specification policies that data connections must respect when moving data between systems

extractedEntities.name: Change Data Capture
extractedEntities.description: Technical mechanism for tracking incremental data changes at the source to enable efficient synchronization without full data re-ingestion

extractedEntities.name: Virtual Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Data connection approach that virtualizes access to source data rather than physically copying or moving it, providing query-time access

keyInsights: Data connections are the critical first step in data integration, handling diverse source types (structured, unstructured, semi-structured, batch, streaming) with different update frequencies and governance policies
Connections can be established via direct secure egress pipelines or through deployed data connection agents when direct connections pose security concerns
Data connections must handle uncertain connectivity to source systems and recover gracefully when sources come back online
The diversity of data types, hosting environments, update frequencies, and governance policies makes data connection far more complex than simple data transfer
Change data capture is a key mechanism enabling efficient incremental synchronization without full re-ingestion of source data

title: Data Protection in Palantir Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's enterprise platform that lets organizations integrate data in a secure environment with access controls governing how users see and interact with data, while complying with data protection laws like GDPR and CCPA

extractedEntities.name: Data Protection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's comprehensive set of technical and organizational controls enabling compliance with data protection regulations including GDPR and CCPA

extractedEntities.name: Purpose-Based Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Access control mechanism where business users specify the purpose of data processing at ingestion and compliance users review the legitimacy and minimize access to sensitive data

extractedEntities.name: Data Governance Infrastructure
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's built-in infrastructure allowing business and compliance teams to collaborate directly within the platform, with rules dynamically enforced and attached to the data itself

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's capability to track the complete history and transformations of data, enabling organizations to map data flows and understand what data exists where and for what purposes

extractedEntities.name: Data Minimization
extractedEntities.description: Data protection principle ensuring that only the minimum necessary data is accessible for a specific purpose, enforced through Foundry's governance controls

extractedEntities.name: Purpose Specification
extractedEntities.description: Requirement that the purpose of data processing be specified at ingestion time, enabling compliance teams to review and enforce data protection policies

extractedEntities.name: Compliance-Integrated Workflow
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where compliance approval processes are tracked directly in the platform alongside the data they govern, rather than in disconnected external systems

keyInsights: Foundry resolves the tension between enterprise data platforms (breaking down silos) and data protection law (data minimization) by embedding compliance workflows directly into the platform
Purpose specification at data ingestion time enables dynamic enforcement of data protection rules attached to the data itself, rather than relying on external disconnected compliance processes
Data lineage capabilities allow organizations to answer fundamental regulatory questions about what data flows where, organizationally and geographically, and for what purposes

Bringing compliance users into the same platform as business users reduces friction and prevents scenarios where rules are not reliably followed or sensitive information is accidentally shared during approval processes
Foundry's data protection capabilities were developed through close partnership with customers and are designed to comply with complex regulations like GDPR and CCPA

title: Data Streaming: Real-time data for real-time decisions (Palantir RFx Blog Series, #8)

extractedEntities.name: Data Streaming
extractedEntities.description: The set of capabilities needed to process real-time information within a data ecosystem, including collection, ingestion, transformation, modeling, contextualization, write-back, analysis, and actions

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that contextualizes streaming data within the broader data ecosystem using the Ontology as a dynamic semantic layer

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's dynamic semantic layer that contextualizes real-time streaming data alongside historical data, enabling operators to make decisions based on both real-time and historical context

extractedEntities.name: Stream Contextualization
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of enriching real-time streaming data with historical context and broader data ecosystem information through the Ontology layer

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Source
extractedEntities.description: Data sources differentiated from batch sources by their higher update frequency, lower volume per update, and time-series nature with each new datum representing the latest state

extractedEntities.name: Real-Time Decision Making
extractedEntities.description: Business-critical use cases requiring low-latency data processing such as halting production after defect detection, adjusting airline crew schedules, or suspending accounts for suspicious transactions

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Write-Back
extractedEntities.description: Capability to push decisions and actions back to source systems in real-time based on streaming data analysis and automated or human decision-making

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Data Governance
extractedEntities.description: Access controls and governance policies applied to real-time streaming data with the same rigor as batch data within the platform

keyInsights: Data streaming encompasses far more than just ingestion — it includes collection, transformation, modeling, contextualization, write-back, analysis, and actions on real-time data

The Ontology serves as the critical semantic layer that contextualizes streaming data alongside historical data, enabling operators to make decisions with full context rather than isolated real-time signals
Streaming data sources differ from batch sources in update frequency, volume per update, and time-series nature where each datum represents the latest state of an entity
Effective streaming requires the same governance controls as batch data, including access controls, lineage tracking, and purpose specification applied to real-time data flows

The gap between streaming promises ('gigabyte throughput', 'millisecond latencies') and real-world implementation is significant, requiring organizations to carefully define their specific latency requirements per use case

title: Defensive Databases: Optimizing Index-Refresh Semantics

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: One of Palantir's core platforms that depends on the Foundations infrastructure layer including Elasticsearch for storage and search

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's autonomous continuous delivery platform that depends on the Foundations infrastructure layer for reliable operation

extractedEntities.name: Gotham
extractedEntities.description: One of Palantir's core platforms backed by the Foundations storage infrastructure including Elasticsearch

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's AI platform that depends on the Foundations infrastructure layer for storage and compute

extractedEntities.name: Elasticsearch Infrastructure
extractedEntities.description: Open-source distributed search and indexing engine operated by Palantir's Foundations group across approximately 300 clusters in containerized and air-gapped environments

extractedEntities.name: Defensive Programming
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of customizing infrastructure software to be more resilient through mechanisms like attribution, rate-limiting, and detailed telemetry without forking source code

extractedEntities.name: Index Refresh Semantics
extractedEntities.description: Elasticsearch mechanism controlling when indexed documents become visible for search, where poorly chosen refresh policies can cause platform instability

extractedEntities.name: Plugin-Based Customization
extractedEntities.description: Approach to making infrastructure software more defensive via plugins rather than forking the source code, enabling upgrades while maintaining customizations

keyInsights: Palantir's Foundations group operates approximately 300 Elasticsearch clusters across containerized, non-containerized, and air-gapped environments serving government and commercial workloads
All Palantir platforms (Foundry, Apollo, Gotham, AIP) depend on a shared infrastructure layer managed by the Foundations organization for storage and compute

Palantir customizes infrastructure software like Elasticsearch through plugins rather than forking source code, enabling them to maintain defensive modifications while staying current with upstream releases
Poorly chosen index refresh policies in Elasticsearch can cascade into platform-wide instability, motivating defensive rewrites of these policies via custom plugins

title: Deploying Across Security Domains

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's autonomous constraint-based continuous delivery platform that deploys software into unclassified and classified air-gapped environments

extractedEntities.name: Binary Transfer Service
extractedEntities.description: Apollo capability (BTS) that autonomously coordinates product and product metadata delivery across security domains, replacing manual sneakernet processes

extractedEntities.name: Air-Gapped Deployment
extractedEntities.description: Deploying software into physically segmented classified networks that cannot connect to the public internet, requiring specialized transfer mechanisms

extractedEntities.name: Cross Domain Solution
extractedEntities.description: Hardware/software systems that permit data to pass between security domains (e.g., unclassified to classified) but do not natively ensure efficient or DevOps-friendly transfer

extractedEntities.name: Constraint-Based Continuous Delivery
extractedEntities.description: Apollo's approach to CD where deployment decisions are driven by environment-specific constraints (security classification, network connectivity, compliance) rather than a single universal pipeline

extractedEntities.name: Security Domain
extractedEntities.description: A logically or physically segmented network operating at a specific security classification level, requiring controlled mechanisms for software delivery across domain boundaries

extractedEntities.name: Sneakernet
extractedEntities.description: Legacy manual process of physically carrying software on DVDs/media between security domains, involving multiple scanning and verification steps

keyInsights: Apollo's Binary Transfer Service autonomously coordinates software delivery across security domains, replacing the traditional sneakernet process of burning DVDs and physically walking them between networks
Air-gapped deployment is a fundamental requirement for national security, defense, and highly-regulated environments where networks cannot connect to the public internet
Cross Domain Solutions enable data passage between security domains but lack native DevOps support, requiring Apollo to layer intelligent delivery orchestration on top

Traditional cross-domain software delivery was so time-intensive that some companies forked codebases into public and private versions, causing government users to fall behind on features and security patches
Apollo uses constraint-based delivery that adapts to each environment's specific security classification, connectivity, and compliance requirements

title: Enabling Responsible AI in Palantir Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's operating system for the modern organization that provides infrastructure for developing, evaluating, deploying, and maintaining AI/ML models throughout their lifecycle

extractedEntities.name: Responsible AI
extractedEntities.description: Multi-dimensional approach to AI encompassing safety, reliability, explainability, governance, and the principle that models should be transparently scoped to specific business or operational problems

extractedEntities.name: Model Management
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's first-class capability for managing AI/ML models across their entire lifecycle including development, evaluation, deployment, monitoring, and maintenance

extractedEntities.name: ModelOps
extractedEntities.description: Operational pattern for managing the full lifecycle of machine learning models including development, deployment, monitoring, feedback loops, and long-term maintenance

extractedEntities.name: Model Lifecycle
extractedEntities.description: The complete lifecycle of an AI/ML model within Foundry spanning development, evaluation, deployment, operational use, feedback collection, and iterative improvement

extractedEntities.name: AI Governance
extractedEntities.description: Controls ensuring responsible AI use including purpose scoping, interdisciplinary oversight, model explainability, and continuous monitoring within Foundry's governance framework

extractedEntities.name: Interdisciplinary AI Development
extractedEntities.description: Pattern requiring engineers, domain experts, data scientists, compliance teams, and other stakeholders to collaborate on AI solutions to ensure they meet specialized domain requirements

extractedEntities.name: AI-Powered Supply Chain Optimization
extractedEntities.description: Using Foundry's AI/ML capabilities to stabilize consumer goods supply chains, optimize manufacturing processes, and monitor public health outbreaks

keyInsights: Responsible AI at Palantir encompasses safety, reliability, explainability, and governance across the entire model lifecycle — not just at the point of model development or deployment
Foundry's model management provides first-class capabilities spanning the complete MLOps lifecycle, integrating AI/ML with the broader platform ecosystem
AI development is treated as an interdisciplinary process requiring collaboration between engineers, domain experts, data scientists, and compliance teams
A model's intended use should be clearly and transparently scoped to specific business or operational problems, recognizing that AI is not the solution to every problem
Dynamic feedback loops and continuous monitoring are essential for sustaining effective model use in practice, not just initial deployment

title: Escaping the Cargo Cult: Exchanging blind faith for real-world outcomes

extractedEntities.name: Outcome-Driven Software
extractedEntities.description: Architectural philosophy that software systems should be evaluated and optimized based on measurable real-world outcomes rather than self-referential process metrics

extractedEntities.name: Operational Connectivity
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of connecting analytics directly to operational decisions so that insights drive tangible actions rather than remaining as static reports or dashboards

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform positioned as an operating system for organizations that connects data, analytics, and operational decisions to drive measurable real-world outcomes

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's semantic layer that models real-world business concepts and relationships, enabling the connection between data insights and operational actions

extractedEntities.name: Cargo Cult Pattern
extractedEntities.description: Anti-pattern in enterprise software where organizations perform the rituals of digital transformation (data lakes, dashboards, KPIs) without achieving measurable real-world outcomes

extractedEntities.name: Enterprise Digital Transformation
extractedEntities.description: The challenge of aligning software procurement, implementation, and operations with actual organizational outcomes rather than performative progress metrics

keyInsights: The 'cargo cult' in enterprise software manifests as self-referential processes across engineering, procurement, implementation, and operations that prioritize internal metrics over real-world outcomes
Software companies, procurement professionals, consultants, IT teams, and financial analysts all tend toward easier-to-achieve metrics rather than focusing on core mission outcomes
Palantir positions its platform as the forcing function that aligns all activity toward measurable operational outcomes rather than performative progress
The misalignment between B2B SaaS value chain activities and actual organizational needs is identified as a root cause of stagnation in enterprise digital transformation

title: From Human-Defined to Software-Defined Data Integration — Part I

extractedEntities.name: Software-Defined Data Integration
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's approach to automating data integration by dynamically generating pipelines from source metadata rather than requiring manual human-written code for each integration

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that implements software-defined data integration to automate and accelerate the historically manual process of integrating data from diverse source systems

extractedEntities.name: First Principles Data Integration
extractedEntities.description: Philosophy of integrating from the most granular source data rather than preprocessed data, ensuring maximum flexibility for downstream applications

extractedEntities.name: Metadata-Driven Automation
extractedEntities.description: Using source system metadata to power dynamic data pipeline generation, automating the automation of data integration

extractedEntities.name: Extensible Knowledge Framework
extractedEntities.description: A framework for encoding data integration knowledge as product that compounds over time, making it easy to contribute and review domain knowledge

extractedEntities.name: Data Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Code-based transformation of raw data from source systems into integrated, harmonized forms — traditionally human-written but increasingly auto-generated through SDDI

keyInsights: Software-Defined Data Integration was born from the realization that manual, human-written data pipelines were error-prone, time-consuming, and required constant maintenance as source data structures changed
The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated SDDI development by creating urgent demand for organizations to integrate data in hours rather than the months traditional approaches required

Three guiding philosophies emerged: integrate from first principles (most granular source data), automate as much as possible (metadata-driven pipeline generation), and build an extensible knowledge framework (learnings compound over time)
SDDI uses source system metadata as a first-class input to dynamically generate integration pipelines, effectively automating the automation process itself

title: From Human-Defined to Software-Defined Data Integration — Part II, Data Discovery

extractedEntities.name: Data Discovery
extractedEntities.description: SDDI capability enabling users to explore, search, and preview data sources across vast data landscapes using metadata-defined transformations and workflow-specific data exploration

extractedEntities.name: Software-Defined Data Integration
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's automated data integration approach that uses metadata from source systems to enable discovery, transformation, and operationalization of enterprise data

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform hosting SDDI capabilities for automated data discovery across ERP, CRM, and other enterprise source systems

extractedEntities.name: Metadata-Defined Transformation
extractedEntities.description: Technique using source system metadata (from ERP, CRM, etc.) to inform how to transform data, revealing content in an intuitive and interactive manner without requiring manual pipeline development

extractedEntities.name: Workflow-Specific Data Exploration
extractedEntities.description: SDDI capability that automatically surfaces and groups relevant datasets for a specified workflow based on years of data integration expertise

extractedEntities.name: Source System Metadata
extractedEntities.description: Metadata residing within common source systems (ERP, CRM) that contains information about data structure and relationships, used by SDDI to drive automated discovery and transformation

keyInsights: Data discovery is the critical challenge of finding the right data across vast enterprise landscapes — organizations know they have the data but cannot locate it when needed most
SDDI uses source system metadata from ERP and CRM systems as a 'Rosetta Stone' to decipher and preview data sources without requiring manual pipeline development
Workflow-specific data exploration automatically surfaces and groups distributed datasets needed for a specified business workflow, drawing on years of encoded integration expertise
The platform abstracts away technical barriers through point-and-click interfaces, shifting user attention from 'where is the data?' to 'how can we use it?'

title: From Human-Defined to Software-Defined Data Integration — Part III, Data Pipelines

extractedEntities.name: Software-Defined Data Integration
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's SDDI approach that generates complete data pipelines out-of-the-box by intelligently reading source data shape and change data capture artifacts

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform hosting SDDI capabilities including automated pipeline generation, metadata inference, and data harmonization

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Generation
extractedEntities.description: SDDI capability that generates complete, efficient data pipelines automatically by reading source data shape and adapting to source system specifications with built-in data health and accuracy checks

extractedEntities.name: Metadata Inference
extractedEntities.description: SDDI capability that learns from source data shape across the entire connected data landscape and adapts dynamic pipeline generation to business-specific customizations without additional maintenance

extractedEntities.name: Data Harmonization
extractedEntities.description: SDDI capability that determines or recommends matches between datasets across different source systems (Oracle NetSuite, SAP S/4HANA, Salesforce) to combine hundreds of sources into a single semantic layer

extractedEntities.name: Semantic Layer
extractedEntities.description: The unified data layer produced by SDDI's harmonization of disparate sources into a coherent, queryable representation of business entities and relationships

extractedEntities.name: Change Data Capture Artifacts
extractedEntities.description: Source system artifacts that SDDI reads to understand incremental data changes and adapt pipeline generation to source system specifications

extractedEntities.name: Entity Resolution
extractedEntities.description: SDDI capability that recommends matches between entities across different source systems, enabling users to confirm or reject recommendations for combining disparate sources

keyInsights: SDDI generates complete production-ready pipelines automatically by reading source data shape and change data capture artifacts, eliminating the need for manual pipeline development
Metadata inference allows SDDI to learn from source data shapes across the entire connected data landscape and adapt dynamically to business-specific customizations without manual intervention
Data harmonization can combine hundreds of disparate sources (Oracle NetSuite, SAP S/4HANA, Salesforce) into a single semantic layer through automated entity resolution with user confirmation

Traditional manual pipeline development is described as slow, messy, and expensive — requiring engineers to understand relationships between tens or hundreds of source systems and constantly adapt to schema changes
SDDI ships generated pipelines with source-specific cleaning logic and honors locale settings of source systems automatically

title: Hardening Palantir's Kubernetes Infrastructure with Cilium

extractedEntities.name: Rubix
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's Kubernetes infrastructure built for streamlining/scaling software platform deployment and strengthening security posture

extractedEntities.name: Cilium Network Policy
extractedEntities.description: Open-source eBPF-based networking and security tool used by Palantir to enforce granular network controls on containerized infrastructure beyond traditional Layer 3/4 firewalls

extractedEntities.name: Container Network Security
extractedEntities.description: Security approach for containerized infrastructure that maintains identity awareness for ephemeral microservices beyond traditional IP/hostname-based controls

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's deployment platform that manages and deploys software onto the Rubix Kubernetes infrastructure

extractedEntities.name: Identity-Aware Network Controls
extractedEntities.description: Network security approach using Cilium that enforces policies based on service identity rather than ephemeral IP addresses or hostnames in containerized environments

extractedEntities.name: Defense in Depth
extractedEntities.description: Security pattern combining network controls, endpoint detection, and telemetry-based alerting across containerized infrastructure layers

extractedEntities.name: eBPF-Based Network Enforcement
extractedEntities.description: Technology underlying Cilium that enables Layer 7 (application-layer) network visibility and control for containerized microservices

keyInsights: Palantir built its own Kubernetes infrastructure called Rubix with dual goals of streamlining software deployment and strengthening security posture
Traditional Layer 3/4 firewalls and EDR tools are insufficient for containerized environments where services are ephemeral and identity cannot be maintained through IP addresses or hostnames alone
Cilium provides identity-aware network controls using eBPF that enable Layer 7 application-layer visibility and enforcement for microservices communication
Palantir's InfoSec team uses Cilium's logging telemetry as a detection source for authoring alerting strategies targeting theoretical attack primitives specific to Kubernetes infrastructure
Containerized EDR challenges include agents treating each ephemeral container as a discrete entity with its own identity, creating noise when services spin up and down frequently

title: Hardware Selection and Logistics (Passwordless Authentication Series, #1)

extractedEntities.name: FIDO2 Authentication
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's next-generation authentication standard combining WebAuthn and CTAP for hardware-backed passwordless authentication using YubiKeys across all platforms

extractedEntities.name: Hardware Authenticator
extractedEntities.description: Physical security key (YubiKey) providing FIDO2 passwordless authentication with FIPS 140-2 validated cryptography and physical presence attestation

extractedEntities.name: Zero Trust Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's network defense philosophy requiring device-level VPN connection before accessing corporate resources, combined with mandatory multi-factor authentication

extractedEntities.name: Phishing-Resistant Authentication
extractedEntities.description: FIDO2's core security property that eliminates credential phishing, social engineering, and interception of authentication material through cryptographic hardware binding

extractedEntities.name: Multi-Factor Authentication
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's mandatory strong MFA enforced company-wide since 2012 and extended to all cloud-hosted customers in 2015, evolved from TOTP/app-based to hardware-backed FIDO2

extractedEntities.name: Defense in Depth
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's security architecture combining hardened network boundaries, zero trust, mandatory MFA, full-tunnel VPN, and assume-breach mentality

extractedEntities.name: WebAuthn
extractedEntities.description: Web authentication standard that is part of FIDO2, providing browser-based cryptographic authentication without passwords

extractedEntities.name: CTAP
extractedEntities.description: Client to Authenticator Protocol, part of FIDO2, enabling communication between hardware authenticators and client devices

keyInsights: Palantir enforced mandatory strong multi-factor authentication company-wide since 2012 and for all cloud-hosted customers since 2015, evolving to hardware-backed FIDO2 as the strongest available modern authentication

FIDO2 with hardware authenticators eliminates risks from weak/compromised/reused credentials, phishing of passwords and secondary factors (TOTP, app-based authenticators), and ensures physical presence via unspoofable attestation
Palantir's zero trust implementation is more restrictive than most — requiring full-tunnel VPN before allowing any access to corporate resources, rather than allowing general internet connectivity
The security architecture assumes all devices are potentially hostile or compromised, designing controls with an assume-breach mentality even within hardened network boundaries
Hardware authenticators also optionally enable cryptographic commit signing, SSH authentication, and cross-platform authentication from a single device

title: How Palantir Foundry Extends Your Data Platforms

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's operating system that extends traditional data platforms into operational connectivity, closing the loop between data, analytics, and operational decisions

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's dynamic semantic layer that models real-world business concepts and relationships, enabling the connection between data insights and operational actions

extractedEntities.name: Operational Connectivity
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's core architectural pattern of connecting analytics to operations continuously and dynamically, enabling decisions to be simulated, implemented, and fed back for improvement

extractedEntities.name: Model Integration
extractedEntities.description: First-class Foundry capability spanning the entire MLOps lifecycle including developing, discovering, managing, and deploying models with native integrations to AWS SageMaker, Azure ML, DataRobot, and Databricks

extractedEntities.name: Decision Orchestration
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of coordinating decisions across functions that are both dynamic (agile to changing conditions) and scalable (optimized across teams, regions, and business units)

extractedEntities.name: Decision Simulation
extractedEntities.description: Foundry capability enabling organizations to simulate the impact of decisions before implementing them in underlying operational systems

extractedEntities.name: Write-Back
extractedEntities.description: Foundry capability enabling decisions made in the platform to be written back to underlying operational source systems, closing the operational loop

extractedEntities.name: Dynamic Semantic Layer
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology's role as a continuously evolving model of business reality that adapts as data, relationships, and decisions change over time

keyInsights: Foundry is explicitly positioned as an operating system — not a data platform — designed to coordinate the interplay of data, models, and decisions across an enterprise
The Ontology serves as a dynamic semantic layer that goes beyond traditional data platforms by connecting analytics directly to operational decisions and enabling write-back to source systems
Foundry extends data platforms in four key ways: model/AI integration, dynamic ontologies, modular workflows, and decision orchestration

Traditional data platforms (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) are ideal for consolidating data and deriving insights, but Foundry adds the operational loop of decision simulation, implementation, and feedback-driven improvement
Model integration in Foundry spans the entire MLOps lifecycle with native integrations to AWS SageMaker, Azure ML, DataRobot, and Databricks, as well as direct code/library import

title: How Palantir Foundry Helps Customers Build and Deploy AI-Powered Decision-Making Applications

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's end-to-end modular and interoperable platform for building and deploying AI-powered decision-making applications, deployed on AWS infrastructure

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's semantic modeling layer that structures data into business-meaningful objects and relationships, enabling AI models to operate on and be understood through business concepts

extractedEntities.name: Amazon SageMaker Integration
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's native integration with AWS SageMaker enabling customers to develop, train, and deploy ML models using SageMaker while leveraging Foundry's operational infrastructure

extractedEntities.name: Operational AI
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of operationalizing AI models as tools that generate consistent long-term business value, connected to trustworthy data foundations and decision infrastructure rather than remaining as isolated experiments

extractedEntities.name: Model Deployment
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's capability to deploy AI/ML models from development into operational workflows where they inform real-time decisions, with feedback loops for continuous improvement

extractedEntities.name: Vaccine Supply Chain
extractedEntities.description: Use case where Palantir Foundry powered by AWS accelerated clinical research and powered vaccine supply chain logistics during the COVID-19 response

extractedEntities.name: Electric Grid Optimization
extractedEntities.description: Use case where Palantir Foundry improved the safety and reliability of the electric grid through AI-powered monitoring and decision-making

extractedEntities.name: AWS
extractedEntities.description: Amazon Web Services cloud infrastructure on which Foundry is deployed, utilizing S3, EC2, and KMS services as the underlying compute and storage layer

keyInsights: 85% of traditional AI/ML projects were predicted to deliver erroneous outcomes, largely because models remain experiments disconnected from operational workflows and trustworthy data foundations
Effective AI requires three things: a trustworthy data foundation, a representation of an institution's decisions (the Ontology), and infrastructure to learn from every decision made

Foundry's modular and interoperable architecture avoids common AI failure modes: siloed development, static data pulls, undocumented handoffs, piecemeal deployment on legacy systems, and lack of accountability to business outcomes
Foundry deploys on AWS utilizing S3, EC2, and KMS services, with native SageMaker integration for model development and training
Real-world deployments span pharmaceutical clinical research (Sanofi), public health research (NIH), energy optimization (bp), and vaccine supply chains
